change.item: TEI namespace bei tei:hi bold entfernt und tei: bei tei:hi auch.

div.head: H區窟龕
div.p: 卧佛院位於四川省安岳縣城北40公里的八廟鄉八廟村，本卷爲有關卧佛院刻經的最後一卷，討論H區，亦包含鄰接的、隸屬遂寧市安居區的菩薩岩。H區位於1962年建成的跑馬灘水庫西北，水庫延伸到卧佛院核心區域以東，即有卧佛造像的南北崖區域以東。崖谷與水庫相交處有一碼頭。北側則有一處較短的溝谷，沿着自西向東的方向下沉。自此溝壑與水面相接處開始，水面的峭壁向北轉彎，其後呈弧形向東延伸（見本卷封三地圖）。H區石窟隱没於這片峭壁茂密的植被中（和）。以前，乘船前往此區最爲便捷。如今，一條柏油馬路通往H區的最東端（）。峭壁頂部有稱作“月亮坪”的平頂小山丘，本卷以此名指代整個H區。

月亮坪區總長600米左右，與卧佛院山谷南北崖核心區域等長。但南北崖區域從1號窟至最後一窟94號窟的步行距離僅爲260米。月亮坪區的窟龕已編録36個（見本卷封三地圖）。石窟的形制通常爲方形窟，高、寬、深的標準尺寸各2米左右。其中4個石窟（95-1號窟、95-3號窟、115號窟和117號窟）的大小僅爲標準尺寸敞口平頂窟的一半，後壁高僅1米左右。這些石窟不適合刊刻經文，可能用作瘞窟。月亮坪僅有兩個石窟刊刻經文，此兩窟爲雙窟，109號窟位於西南側，110號窟位於東北側（）。另有97號窟、105號窟、106-1號窟、114號窟及121號窟5個窟龕僅部分開鑿。116號窟、118號窟和119號窟原計劃刊刻經文，後改成雕鑿造像（；和）。116號窟有一則南宋紹興二年（1132）題刻（和）。除了佛教圖像以外，119號窟還有道教圖像。
石窟群始於西側的95-1、95-2及95-3號窟，編録於2012年。95-3號窟所在的石塊最初位於95-2號窟東北27米處。此石已崩塌，窟頂朝下，窟口朝向西北方向8米開外的山壁。再向前25.5米處是峭壁上的95號窟和96號窟（）。這兩個窟以及97號窟在2019年6月9日凌晨3點崩塌（）。感謝卧佛院文物保護員吴忠富先生於2024年1月3日告知。

靠近水岸的97號窟之後爲98號窟至104-1號窟一排石窟。它們或三窟一組，或雙窟并列。98號窟、99號窟和100號窟三窟爲一組，101和102號窟爲雙窟。103號窟、104號窟和104-1號窟又構成三窟組窟。102號窟和103號窟周圍有一排小孔，延伸到峭壁上緣，此處應當有建築物或脚手架位。其後約15米開外由一陡峭石坡隔離而成104-2號窟和104-3號窟。標記此二窟位置，見成都文物考古研究所第354頁和第356頁之間的地圖；秦臻、張雪芬、雷玉華第39頁，而正文未提及。吴忠富先生於2006年釣魚時目睹了這兩個石窟的坍塌。106號窟爲單獨開鑿（），由於其位於峭壁更高處，陡峭的岩石將其與其他石窟分隔（）。此窟鄰接未完工的106-1號窟。
雙窟109號窟和110號窟爲刻經窟，位於從107號窟至113號窟這排石窟之中，距離碼頭約280米。此地被跑馬灘水庫淹没前有丁姓人家居住於此，因此叫作“丁家岩”。
再向東60米左右爲114號龕，龕室窄小，僅120厘米高，内有兩尊未完成的造像雛形。繼續向東爲呈標準尺寸的115號窟。2024年首次編號的115-1號窟也是一個敞口平頂標準尺寸窟，發現於115號窟東北方向的新建階梯旁，d壁因采石而損毁（）。

115號窟和116號窟之間是位於西南的安岳縣與位於東北的遂寧市安居區的行政分界綫。116號窟内有一座精美的浮雕寶塔（116-3號），以及H區字數最多的題刻（116-2號），爲發願文。與此窟接鄰的117號窟壁面平整，顯示該窟已經完工，但尺寸僅爲標準窟的一半（）。隨後爲有兩軀菩薩立像的118號窟（）、有佛教與道教造像的119號窟、保存較好的120號窟、尚未完工的121號窟以及最後的122號窟。由121號窟和122號窟可以推測石窟開鑿的過程：先標記窟口輪廓，再從石窟下方開始鑿起（）；一旦窟口完成，再繼續往深一層的下部開鑿（）。

figure.head: 從卧佛院南崖東端遠眺月亮坪。2010年拍攝
Moon Plateau viewed from the eastmost point of the southern cliff of the Grove; photograph taken in 2010

div.head: 雙經窟
div.p: 107號窟至113號窟近乎位於同一水平綫上，開鑿於高出夏天水位十餘米的陡崖（）。107號窟至110號窟下方有天然突起的岩塊，形同壁間石道，可用以連通這4個石窟（和）。其中，109號窟和110號窟雙經窟（）。

109號窟和110號窟雙窟并排，僅一壁之隔（和），兩窟形制、尺寸相同，而且窟底與窟頂皆齊平（）。與北崖的雙窟1號窟和2號窟相同，109號窟和110號窟亦僅刊刻一部佛經。1號窟和2號窟刊刻《妙法蓮華經》，計71,000字。109號窟和110號窟刊刻《大方便佛報恩經》。《大方便佛報恩經》T#156，3：124a17－154b13。此經七卷九品，計64,242字，窟内僅刊刻五卷七品。相比1號窟和2號窟六壁滿刻經文，109號和110號組窟僅刊刻五壁，且止於110號窟e壁中部，此窟有足够空間刊刻完其餘兩卷，不知爲何原因工匠未能滿刻經文。

figure.head: 107－110號窟位置關係和當地幫手。2010年拍攝
Caves 107–110 and the local workers; photograph taken in 2010

div.p: 《大方便佛報恩經》是“關於佛陀與菩薩如何知恩、報恩的本緣故事集”，知恩、報恩“正是菩薩道本身” 。見本卷歐鋭恩（Ryan Richard Overbey）一文，和。

此部刻經始於109號窟d壁的序品。此壁有一處高、寬各190厘米左右的正方形打磨區域，其界格精確，且比例恰當。十行的寬度和十個字的高度均爲20.5厘米。刻工於此壁刊刻7,347字。若繼續以此尺寸刊刻，則總計64,242字的全部經文無法在此雙窟六壁刻完。因此，繼而刊刻正壁e壁時，刻者在第16行後弃用界格，刻字間距變得緊密，同時，f壁上十個字的高度减到14.2厘米左右。書丹者唯恐雙窟壁面無法容納全部經文，衹有這樣方可在壁面安排更多的經文。
109號窟（）刊刻了半部《大方便佛報恩經》，其中卷四始於f壁後部末端。110號窟d壁順延刊刻，由於109號窟f壁及110號窟d壁的前部均有所損壞，因此無法確定刻經的確切銜接處。并且這兩壁刻經横排未對齊，亦無法統計毁損部分各行刻經的字數。
此經刊刻終止於110號窟正壁e壁中部卷五第七品。雖然打磨區域覆蓋整個壁面，但是狹窄的刻經區止於距f壁89厘米處的第52行。刊刻者似有意繼續刊刻最後兩卷，但不知何故未能實現。110號窟f壁未作打磨，未經刊刻。
刻經者基本上有一套系統的經文卷次和品次的標識。在已刻七品每品的品首皆有經題、品名和品次，品末未另外標出。 但有一處例外，即第六品起始處的“大方便佛報恩經惡友品第［六］，四，子”（109號窟f壁第13A段第70行），不僅有卷次“四”，還有一“子”字，標識更多信息。

figure.head: 雙刻經窟109號窟和110號窟平面圖。雷魯卡根據馬本漢和王平國田野繪圖製作
Ground plan of twin sutra Caves 109 and 110; drawing by Lukas Ledderose after field map by MB and MS

div.p: 各卷結尾處應該均注明了經題及卷次，而目前僅在卷三末完整保存經題及卷次“大方便佛報恩經卷第三”（109號窟f壁第13D段第69行），我們通過類比復原其他四卷末的標識。

此刻經采用本子的特别之處是，上述的衍文“子”字（109號窟f壁第13A段第70行）。此字緊跟上文提及的第六品起始處的經文標識，其字體和刻法與刻經相同，恰好位於此行正中間，在經文標識之後空四格，在經文正文之前空一格。此字的作用可能是表示此經帙號。然而，現存《大藏經》所收此經的編號并非此字。例如，智昇（669－740年左右）《開元釋教録略出》根據《千字文》將此經置於“器”字之下。《開元釋教録略出》，T#2155,55：732a26－28。其他經録將此經列入“覆”、“器”（依照智昇）、“墨”、“心”等字之下。詳情見。這裏“心”字并非取自《千字文》，而來自佛教偈頌，用於標識敦煌一座寺院的佛典。方廣錩。卧佛院僅於《大方便佛報恩經》出現疑似帙號的佛經標識，很難追溯其源。

div.head: 題刻
div.p: H區所見三則題刻分别位於109號窟、116號窟和119號窟。109號窟e壁（刻經起首之前，1C段）的題刻云： ……龍開十……這則殘存題刻或許是供養人或工匠的名字。
116號窟d壁的較長題刻（116-2號；和）在三則題刻中保存最完好，紀年爲南宋紹興二年。此題刻爲發願文，提及“重粧”正壁三尊佛像（）。當此壁和f壁七級浮雕塔（116-3號；本卷扉頁和）告成之後，此窟必定蔚爲壯觀，更吸引當地香客。

116號窟題刻可能是宋代當地信徒尊奉《道行般若經》佛教傳統依照慧琳（736－820）《一切經音義》將“般若”（梵文： prajñā ）讀作 bore （《一切經音義》T#2128，54：313c8－9），一些歷史音韵學字典亦視之爲歷史上的正確讀音（蒲立本第40頁；《古漢語字典》第38頁），現代漢語字典也頒訂其爲“規範”的讀音，本卷因此遵循此傳統與規範將其轉寫爲 bore 。不過，學界對此發音有所争論。伯希和（Paul Pelliot， 1878－1945）對此早已提出質疑，他認爲該詞的字源是古印度土語（Prâkrit）的* paññā （巴利語；參較犍陀羅語： praṃña 和 praña 等等)，“般”讀 ban 并没有錯。伯希和第274－275頁。本卷所引用的石刻材料顯示，“般若”在6世紀初即常被轉寫作“波若”，傳世經本亦有此傳統，“般若”的發音在歷史上是否與“波若”發生了混淆，有待進一步研究。的一個例證，可惜“若經”兩字風化不清，衹能根據可見的前三字補充，并以灰色字體標記。題刻云：
div.quote: /1/ 粵有普州安岳縣廣德鄉光通 據81號窟宋碑補此“通”字。《中國佛教石經·四川省第五卷》第14頁。里黄貴場内/2/今佛積善弟子劉才順充□□□意爲自身 /3/行，□三十七歲，丙子正月□□日見生，乃於往/4/年八月□□□風疾在身□□遂聞明雜慮 /5/《道行般若經》□□□才順發心於重粧三身 /6/諸佛□□□□□尊像共計三龕。今以聞 /7/見佛□□□□□（像？）以家設奉，伏願□□□ /8/ 佛□□□□□□歲次紹興二年六月庚寅五 /9/日［1132年7月19日］ □□□□劉才順夫婦恭記 /10/□□□□□□□以重粧祈□□□□□□（保？） /11/□□□□□□□□服如初□□□（中？）□□□ /12/□□□□□□（寶？）鑒知……録文參見成都文物考古研究所第398頁，以及秦臻、張雪芬、雷玉華第43頁。

figure.head: 116號窟d壁，116-2號題刻。2010年拍攝
Cave 116, wall d, colophon 116-2; photograph taken in 2010
119號窟d壁題刻。2010年拍攝
Cave 119, wall d, colophon; photograph taken in 2010

div.p: 最後一則題刻見於119號窟d壁（和）：
佛弟子……五十三……故於…… 此則題刻有邊框，其形如碑。題刻左側有較小的錐形壁龕，用於放置燈具；右側是一較大的方形灰身龕，開口處有雙層邊框，可以卡上擋板，以遮蔽灰身龕，而擋板和骨灰現已蕩然無存。

div.head: H區之斷代
div.p: H區的刻經窟無紀年，僅能根據石窟形制以及書法風格作出推測。其餘未刻經窟含H區中唯一確定紀年的南宋紹興二年題刻，但此實爲後代重修造像的願文的時間標記（和），原窟與原雕像亦僅能以石窟建築和造像風格推斷其時代。
南宋紹興二年題刻比卧佛像頭側的題刻尚早兩年（ 和）。後者録文：

/1/潼川瑞䲨攻鎸，母山、/2/男士如（？）、士章、孫□伏覩/3/太原大郎命重修/4/卧像并侍者嵓/5/ 上。宋甲寅紹興/6/初夏望後一日［1134年5月11日］/7/輒記。
紹興二年和紹興四年（1134）這兩則題刻記録了造像的修復工作，應當是給造像翻新着色，由此可證實卧佛院的宗教活動在南宋初期的延續。同一時期還有一則淳熙十年（1183）刻於37號墓塔的題刻（和）。録文見本卷。

紹興二年題刻未提及116號窟正壁造像製作於何時，但根據雕刻風格，H區116號窟、118號窟和119號窟的造像應刻於8世紀中葉。2024年1月2日與中國社會科學院楊筱和四川省文物考古研究院李飛在現場的個人交流。謹此致謝。由於這些造像是在原本準備用於刻經的方形窟中雕鑿的，因此石窟的年代必定早於造像。亦即，H區石窟的年代，包括109號窟和110號刻經窟，與卧佛院南北崖區域中的石窟相近，大約在8世紀20年代至30年代。
對書法風格的深入研究可進一步印證憑藉造像風格所作的斷代。然而，在進行這一推論之前，我們首先要考慮根據書法風格斷代的諸多不確定性： 手書墨迹已不復存在，佚名書丹者的個人風格無從知曉，佛經以標準化楷書書寫，致使幾乎無從展現個人書風，刻工所刻與書丹者所書的差别及其差异程度亦不可確定，以及刻石損泐造成刻經模糊難辨，等等。儘管如此，卧佛院的刻經字體仍然可見明顯的風格差异，同時也存在諸多相似之處。

，并列對比了109號窟d壁無紀年刻經與73號窟d壁 唐開元十五年（727）刻經。73號窟可見經文刊刻如何力求追摹墨迹。所有刻字大小近乎一致，排布均匀。字字横平竪直，如“三”“一”兩字的横畫皆平穩如水平綫。而109號窟刻字的結體則大膽欹仄，如“三”“一”兩字的横畫大都稍微傾斜，竪畫亦是如此，如有兩處“土”字和“即”“名”二字。這些差异表明上述73號窟d壁和109號窟d壁兩壁刻經的書丹者應不是同一人。然而，書法專家一致認爲，無紀年的109號窟d壁的時代亦應爲8世紀20或30年代左右。至於此壁是否早於或晚於刊刻於開元十五年的73號窟，則尚有争議。2023年7月與賴非、向净卿、潘灝賢、劉芊妤，以及2024年5月與沈維進和白冰的個人交流。謹此致謝。

figure.head: 107－109號窟位置關係。2010年拍攝
Spatial relation of Caves 107–109; photograph taken in 2010
108號窟和109號窟位置關係及其下方連通石窟的天然岩道。2010年拍攝
Spatial relation between Caves 108 and 109 and the narrow ledge connecting them below; photograph taken in 2010
懸於水面的95－96號窟。2015年拍攝
Caves 95–96 hanging over water; photograph taken in 2015
109號窟d壁未紀年刻經（左）與73號窟d壁唐開元十五年（727）刻經（右）局部。2012年拍攝

div.head: Caves in Section H
div.p: The Grove of the Reclining Buddha (Wofo Yuan 卧佛院, hereafter: the Grove) is located in Bamiao Village (Bamiao Cun 八廟村) in Bamiao Community (Bamiao Xiang 八廟鄉), 40 kilometers to the north of the county town Anyue (Anyue Xiancheng 安岳縣城). The present volume, our last on the Grove deals with the caves in section H that includes the adjacent Bodhisattva Cliff (Pusayan 菩薩岩) administrated by Anju District (Anju Qu 安居區) of Suining 遂寧 City. The section is situated to the north-west of the artificial Paomatan 跑馬灘 Reservoir, built in 1962, that extends east of the core area of the Grove, which is the valley with the statue of the reclining Buddha. Where the valley meets the lake is a dock. A shorter valley to the north also descends in a west-east direction. From where this valley meets the lake, the lake’s cliff turns toward the north and then continues in an arc further to the east (see map on back end paper). Hidden among lush vegetation on this cliff are the caves of section H (fig. and fig. ). Earlier, they were best reached by boat. Today, an asphalt road leads to the eastmost part of section H (fig. ). One of the flat-topped small hills above the cliff is called Moon Plateau (Yueliang Ping 月亮坪). The name has been applied in the present volume to the entire area, section H.

The total length of the row of caves in the Moon Plateau amounts to some six hundred meters, which is as long as the valley in the core area of the Grove, although there the walk from the first Cave 1 to the last Cave 94 is only 260 meters. 36 caves have been recorded in the Moon Plateau area (see map on back end paper). The normal cave type is box-shaped and is standard-sized with a height, width, and depth measuring about two meters; but four caves (95-1, 95-3, 115, and 117) are of half size where the height of the rear wall is about one meter. Those are not suited to contain sutra texts, but were probably used for burials of deceased persons. Only two caves in the Moon Plateau area contain sutras. They are twin caves, 109 to the southwest, and 110 to the northeast (fig. ). Five caves or niches (97, 105, 106-1, 114 and 121) are hewn only to some extent. In Caves 116, 118, and 119, the plan to carve sutras was abandoned, and sculptures were carved instead (fig. ; fig. and fig. ). Cave 116 has a colophon of 1132 (fig. and fig. ). Alongside Buddhist figures, Cave 119 also contains Daoist imagery.

The row of caves begins in the west with 95-1, 95-2, and 95-3, which were only discovered in 2012. The stone block that houses Cave 95-3, lay originally 27 meters to the north-east of 95-2. It has toppled over and lies upside down with the opening toward the cliff about eight meters to the north-west. Another 25.5 meters onwards follow Caves 95 and 96 high on the cliff (fig. ). These two caves crashed, together with Cave 97, on June 9, 2019, at 3 am (fig. ).We are grateful to the Protector of Cultural Heritage (Wenwu baohu yuan 文物保護員) at the Grove, Mr. Wu Zhongfu 吴忠富, who reported it on January 3, 2024.

figure.head: 由跑馬灘水庫通往107－110號窟的泊船處。2010年拍攝
Landing place of boat on the Paomatan Reservoir to Caves 107–110; photograph taken in 2010

div.p: After Cave 97 near the shore follows a row of caves from 98 to 104-1. They are either a set of triple caves or of twin caves. Caves 98, 99, and 100 are triple caves. Caves 101 and 102 are twin caves. Caves 103, 104, and 104-1 are, again, triple caves. Around Caves 102 and 103 are rows of small holes which reach to the upper edge of the cliff. There must have been architecture or scaffoldings. After circa 15 meters, separated by a steep rocky slope, Caves 104-2 and 104-3 are located.The caves are shown on the maps in , map following 354, and in 39, but are not mentioned in the texts. Mr. Wu Zhongfu witnessed the collapse of the two caves in 2006, while he was fishing. Cave 106 (fig. ) is an isolated cave, since it is higher on the cliff and is separated from other caves by steep rocks (fig. ). It is accompanied by the unfinished Cave 106-1.

The two sutra Caves 109 and 110 are included in a row of seven caves from 107 to 113. Their distance to the dock is about 280 meters. This area is called Ding Family Cliff (Dingjia Yan 丁家岩) because a Ding family lived here before the place was drowned in the Paomatan Reservoir.

figure.head: 107－110號窟（由左至右）的位置關係。2024年拍攝
Spatial relation of Caves 107–110 (from left to right); photograph taken in 2024

div.p: About 60 meters further to the east follows Niche 114, which is shallow and only 120 cm high. It houses the roughcasts of two unfinished sculptures. Further to the east, Cave 115 is again of the standard size. Another standard size cave is found further to the north-east at the newly built stairway. This cave was given number 115-1 in 2024. Its wall d is damaged by a quarry (fig. ).

Between Caves 115 and 116 runs the political boundary between Anyue County on the southwest and Suining City on the northeast. Cave 116 has a fine relief pagoda, 116-3, and the longest colophon in section H, which is the votive inscription 116-2. Next to the polished, obviously finished Cave 117 of half standard size (fig. ), follow Cave 118, with two standing bodhisattva figures (fig. ), Cave 119, with—as mentioned—Buddhist as well as Daoist sculptures, the well preserved Cave 120, the unfinished Cave 121, and finally Cave 122. In Caves 121 and 122 it is possible to detect the process in making a cave: the outline of the entrance was first marked and then the lower part of the cave was chiseled out (fig. ); when the entrance of the cave was done, the lower part of the rear wall was then chiseled out further deeper (fig. ).

figure.head: 109號窟近景。2015年拍攝
A close-up of Cave 109; photograph taken in 2015
107－110號窟（左）至 111號窟（右）位置關係。2010年拍攝
Spatial relation between Caves 107–110 (left) and 111 (right); photograph taken in 2010

div.head: The Two Sutra Caves
div.p: The two sutra Caves 109 and 110 sit in the roughly horizontal row of Caves 107–113 on the steep cliff face about a dozen of meters above the water level in summer (fig. ). A narrow ledge runs directly under Caves 107–110, which could have been used to reach one cave from another. (fig. and fig. ).

Caves 109 and 110 are twin caves (fig. ). Twin caves are positioned side by side and separated by a common wall (fig. ). They have the same shape and size, and both their floors and roofs are on the same level (fig. ). Like the twin Caves 1 and 2 on the northern slope, the twin Caves 109 and 110 also contain only one text. Caves 1 and 2 feature the Lotus Sutra of the Wonderful Law (Miaofa lian­hua jing 妙法蓮華經) with ca. 71,000 characters. Caves 109 and 110 feature the Great Skillful Means Sutra on the Buddha’s Repaying Kindness (Da fang­bian Fo bao’­en jing 大方便佛報恩經; hereafter: Repaying Kindness Sutra).T#156, 3: 124a17–154b13. The text has seven scrolls spanning nine chapters, 64,242 characters in total, but only five scrolls with seven chapters have been carved. Thus, whereas in the twin Caves 1 and 2 all six walls are filled, in Caves 109 and 110 only five walls are filled. The text ends in the middle of wall e in Cave 110. There would have been enough space to finish the remaining two scrolls.

The Repaying Kindness Sutra is “an anthology of Buddhist narratives demonstrating how Buddhas and bodhisattvas can recognize kindness (zhi’en 知恩) and repay kindness (bao’en 報恩)” which is “nothing more or less than the bodhisattva path itself.”See the article “The Buddha’s Repaying Kindness Sutra” by Ryan Richard Overbey in the present volume, pp. and .

The carving begins with the preface on wall d in Cave 109. The wall has a square polished area of ca. 190 cm × 190 cm and a well-proportioned grid; the width of ten columns and the length of ten characters both measure 20.5 cm. The carvers carved 7,347 characters on this wall. If they had continued at this pace, they could not have placed the entire 64,242 characters of the sutra onto the six walls of the two caves. Thus, as early as on the second wall e, they abandoned the grid after column 16. Columns and spaces between characters are now tighter, and on wall f, the length of ten characters is reduced to around 14.2 cm. The calligraphers could thus place more characters onto the available surface. They feared that they would not otherwise be able to fit the entire sutra into the two caves.

figure.head: 雙刻經窟109號和110號窟平面圖。雷魯卡根據馬本漢和王平國田野繪圖製作
Ground plan of twin sutra Caves 109 and 110; drawing by Lukas Ledderose after field map by MB and MS

div.p: The text carved in Cave 109 (fig. ) amounts to half of the entire sutra. Shortly before the end of wall f, scroll 4 begins. The text continues on wall d of Cave 110, but the exact juncture can no longer be determined, because the front parts on wall f of 109 and on wall d of 110 are both damaged. Computation on the basis of the numbers of characters in the columns is no longer possible either, because of the absence of horizontal rows.

In the middle of the rear wall e of Cave 110 the carving stops after the seventh chapter in the fifth scroll. The narrow field of script ends 89 cm from wall f with column 52, although the polished area extends over the entire wall. It thus seems as if the carvers intended to continue with the last two chapters, yet for some reason they did not. Wall f in Cave 110 has not received a final polish and is not carved at all.

Generally speaking, text markers are used systematically. The beginning of each of the carved seven chapters is indicated by its title and chapter number after the sutra title. This is not repeated at the end of each chapter. An exception is the beginning of chapter 6 (大方便佛報恩經惡友品第［六］，四，子; Cave 109 wall f, segment 13A, column 70). It also includes the scroll number 4 and a character, zi 子, which provides additional information.

The end of each of the five scrolls should have been marked by the sutra title followed by the scroll number. Only at the end of scroll 3, however, are all characters still visible (大方便佛報恩經卷第三; Cave 109 wall f, segment 13D, column 69). Again, by analogy we can reconstruct the markings for the other four scrolls.

A special feature of the carved version of the sutra is the additional character, zi 子, following the just mentioned text marker for the beginning of chapter 6. The character is carved in the same calligraphical style and execution as the sutra text. It is well placed in the middle of the column, four spaces after the text marker and one space before the sutra text (Cave 109 wall f, segment 13A, column 70), indicating probably the wrapper number (zhihao 帙號) of the sutra. However, the sutra is not listed in this character among the Buddhist canons known to us. The Digest of the Kaiyuan Era Catalog of Śākyamuni’s Teachings (Kaiyuan shijiao lu lüechu 開元釋教録略出) by Zhisheng 智昇 (ca. 669–740) lists the sutra for instance under the character qi 器 according to the Thousand Character Classic (Qian zi wen 千字文).T#2155,55: 732a26–28.
Other canons list the sutra under the characters fu 覆, qi 器 (following Zhi­sheng), mo 墨 or xin 心.For more details, see . The last character xin was not taken from the Thousand Character Classic, but from a Buddhist verse, which was used for registering the sutras of a canon in a monastery in Dunhuang 敦煌. . It is the only case at the Grove that the Repaying Kindness Sutra has a text marker indicating a wrapper number. It is thus difficult to trace its origin.

div.head: The Colophons
div.p: Three colophons are found in section H, one each in Caves 109, 116, and 119. The characters reading . . . Longkai (or dragon opened) ten . . . ……龍開十…… on wall e in Cave 109 (before the sutra text begins, segment 1C), might be fragments of a short colophon featuring the name of a donor or an artisan.

The lengthy colophon on wall d in Cave 116 is best preserved among the three colophons, and it has a date of 1132 (116-2; fig. and fig. ). It is a votive inscription referring to reembellishing the three Buddha figures on wall e (fig. ). When this was done, in addition to the pagoda sculptured in relief on wall f (116-3, frontispiece and fig. ), the cave must have been magnificent and have attracted more pilgrims from the region.

This colophon could serve as evidence of the local cult of the Perfection of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines ( Aṣṭasāhasrikā­prajñā­pāra­mitā ; Dao­xing bo­re jing 道行般若經)The term “般若” has been interpreted as Skt. prajñā , and therefore pronounced “bore” in Buddhist tradition from Huilin’s 慧琳 (736 – 820) Sound and Meaning of All Sutras (Yiqiejing yinyi 一切經音義) onwards. Some dictionaries of historical phonology also regard “bore” as the historically correct pronunciation of the term. (40; 38.) Modern Chinese lexicons also promulgate “bore” as the “standardized” pronunciation. This volume thus follows the tradition and standard and transliterates it as “bore.” However, this remains a controversial issue among academics. Paul Pelliot (1878–1945) suggested that the etymology of the Chinese term is * paññā ( Pāli ; cf. Gāndhārī : praṃña , praña , etc.) in ancient Indian Prākrit . “般” should therefore be read as “ban.” (274–275.) The stone carved texts quoted in this volume show that “般若” was often transcribed as “波若” from the beginning of the sixth century on. This transcription is also found in the transmitted scriptures. Whether the pronunciation of “般若” has been confused with that of “波若” in history awaits further research. in the Song dynasty. While the first three characters of the title are legible, the last two characters, “re 若” and “jing 經,” are weathered. These illegible characters are supplemented here in grey in accord with those still available [for the sake of completeness]. It reads:

figure.head: 116號窟d壁以白框表示116-2號題刻的位置。2010年拍攝
Cave 116, wall d with Colophon 116-2 encased in white; photograph taken in 2010

div.quote:
Verily, in Pu Prefecture, Anyue County, Guang­de Village, Guangtong Hamlet, in Huang­gui Field, the disciple of the Buddha, who has accumulated the good, Liu Cai­shun . . . intended for his own peaceful conduct, in his thirty-seventh year, a bingzi year, first month, on the . . . day, . . .. On the first day of the eighth month of last year, I have been affected by a contagious disease in my own body . . . whereupon I heard the Perfection of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines ( Aṣṭa­sā­has­rikā Praj­ñā­pā­ra­mitā ) that can clarify mixed worries . . . [Liu] Caishun resolved to reembellish three Buddha statues . . . the venerable statues in a total of three niches. Now, in order to hear and see the Buddha . . . I have brought a statue (?) to my home to venerate. Sincerely we wish . . . Buddha . . .. On the fifth day of the sixth month in the second year of the shaoxing era [July 19, 1132] . . . respectfully inscribed by Liu Caishun and his wife. In order to pray with the new embellishment . . . like in the beginning . . . precious (?) mirror . . ..
/1/ 粵有普州安岳縣廣德鄉光通里黄貴場内/2/今佛積善弟子劉才順充□□□意爲自身 /3/行，□三十七歲，丙子正月□□日見生，乃於往/4/年八月□□□風疾在身□□遂聞明雜慮 /5/《道行般若經》□□□才順發心於重粧三身 /6/諸佛□□□□□尊像共計三龕。今以聞 /7/見佛□□□□□（像？）以家設奉，伏願□□□ /8/ 佛□□□□□□歲次紹興二年六月庚寅五 /9/日［1132年7月19日］ □□□□劉才順夫婦恭記 /10/□□□□□□□以重粧祈□□□□□□（保？） /11/□□□□□□□□服如初□□□（中？）□□□ /12/□□□□□□（寶？）鑒知…… Transcription cf. 398 and 43.
div.p: The last one is the colophon on wall d of Cave 119 (fig. and fig. ). It reads: The Buddhist disciple . . . fifty-three . . . died/thus in/on . . . 佛弟子……五十三……故於……

The colophon has a frame that resembles a stele. To its left, a smaller, cone-shaped niche functions as a lamp container. To the right of the colophon, a larger, rectangular casket is a burial niche for ashes. Its opening has double frames that allows a screen to be stuck to avoid sight into the burial niche. The screen as well as the ashes have not survived to us.

figure.head: 119號窟d壁的灰身龕、燈龕與題刻。2007年拍攝
Cave 119, wall d, detail; a burial niche, a lamp niche, and a colophon; photograph taken in 2007

div.p: The sutra caves in Section H are undated. Attempts can be made based on their shape and calligraphic style. The other caves without sutras contain the only secure dating of 1132, which, however, is of the colophon for a later restoration of sculptures (fig. and fig. ). Their original dating can also only be judged by means of the architecture of the caves and the style of the images.

figure.head: 白框標示17號題刻的位置。2024年拍攝
Colophon 17 encased in white; photograph taken in 2024
卧佛像東邊石壁上的17號題刻。2010年拍攝
Colophon 17 to the east of the head of the Reclining Buddha; photograph taken in 2010

div.p: The colophon of 1132 was carved two years earlier than a colophon next to the head of the colossal statue of the Reclining Buddha statue (fig. and fig. ). That one reads:
div.quote: Carved by the craft Rui Hong from Tongchuan [Prefecture], his mother Shan, his sons Shiru (?) and Shizhang, his grandson [. . .] saw that the Great Gentleman from Taiyuan [in Shanxi] had the image of the Reclining [Buddha] and the attendant [figures] on the cliff restored. I thus recorded this one day after the full moon of the early summer [fourth month] of the jiayin year of the shaoxing era of the Song [May 11, 1134]. /1/潼川瑞䲨攻 鎸，母山、/2/男士如（？）、士章、孫□伏覩/3/太原大郎命重修/4/卧像并侍者嵓/5/ 上。宋甲寅紹興/6/初夏望後一日/7/輒記。

figure.head: Aerial view of the Grove of the Reclining Buddha in 2024; source: Google™Earth
2024年卧佛院航拍圖。采自谷歌地球

div.p: Both colophons, that of 1132 and that of 1134, record the restoration of figures, which probably consisted in refreshing the paint. These carvings thus testify to a continuous religious activity at the Grove in the beginning of the Southern Song. Also to this period belong the colophon of 1183 (the 10th year of the chunxi era 淳熙十年) on the tomb stupa 37 (fig. and fig. ).For a transcription and translation see .

The colophon of 1132 does not tell us, when the figures on wall e in Cave 116 to which it refers were first made. Based on the sculptural style, the figures in Section H (Caves 116, 118, and 119) were made during the mid-8th century.Personal communication with Yang Xiao 楊筱 of the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences 中國社會科學院 and Li Fei 李飛 of the Sichuan Provincial Research Institute of Cultural Relics and Archaeology 四川省文物考古研究院 on January 2, 2024. We are grateful to them for sharing their expertise. Since the figures were later sculpted in the box-shaped caves that were hewn originally for containing carved sutras, the caves were earlier than the figures. It is to say that the caves in Section H including the sutra caves 109 and 110 are from the same time as those of 720s–730s in the valley.

A close look at the calligraphy style confirms the dating based on the style of the figures. Before doing this, we should reflect on the uncertainties of dating carved sutras on the basis of their calligraphic style: the original handwriting in ink is no longer available; the personal style of the anonymous calligrapher cannot be known; sutras have to be written in a standardized regular script (kaishu 楷書), which leaves little room for individual expression; it cannot be ascertained how and to which degree the carver modified the calligrapher’s brush strokes; and the wear and tear of the stone carvings have blurred the visual evidence. Nevertheless, definite stylistic differences exist between the carved characters in the Grove, and similarities can be observed, too.

Fig. juxtaposes details of the undated carved sutra on wall d of Cave 109 and the dated wall d of Cave 73 of the fifteenth year of the kaiyuan 開元 era (727). The latter shows a carefully, correctly executed handwriting. All characters are of almost the same size and well placed in the squares of the grid. The horizontal strokes are strictly horizontal, the vertical ones go precisely from zero to six o’clock. For instance, all the horizontal strokes of san 三 (three) and yi 一 (one) lie calmly like a waterline. By contrast, the structure of the characters is more daring and slanting in Cave 109. For instance, most of the horizontal strokes of san 三 (three) and yi 一 (one) are slightly inclined. The vertical strokes go from zero to seven o'clock like in tu 土 (earth) twice, ji 即 (just) and ming 名 (name). These differences show that the two juxtaposed walls in Cave 73 and Cave 109 were written by two different individuals. Yet regarding the date of wall d of Cave 109, experts of calligraphy agree that it is also from the 720s or 730s. Disputable is whether it is earlier or later than the dated wall of Cave 73 of 727.Personal communications by Lai Fei 賴非, Xiang Jingqing 向净卿, Pan Haoxian 潘灝賢, and Liu Qianyu 劉芊妤 in July, 2023 and by Shen Weijin 沈維進 as well as Bai Bing 白冰 in May, 2024. We are grateful to them for sharing their expertise.
